steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has a long subject: "a unique tradition of embroidery [blank] back over two thousand years — one that includes iconic double-sided stitching... — remains popular." The main verb is "remains." The blank must link "embroidery" to "back over two thousand years" without adding a second finite verb that would compete with "remains."

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'dates': A finite present-tense verb. Using "dates" creates two competing main verbs in one clause — "dates" and "remains" — making the sentence a run-on without a conjunction. ✗
  • Choice B — 'date': Also a finite verb (base form). Same problem as A: it competes with "remains" as a second predicate with no conjunction to join them. ✗
  • Choice C — 'has dated': A finite present-perfect verb. Same structural problem — it creates a second predicate, and "has dated back" is not idiomatic in this context. ✗
  • Choice D — 'dating': A present participle. "Dating back over two thousand years" is a participial phrase that modifies "tradition of embroidery" without adding a second finite verb. The sentence's one main verb remains "remains." ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
